affected_groups: disadvantaged applicants dependent on effective enforcement
content.decision_question: Should equity reform rely on direct admissions or structural mandates that maintainers must follow, or on fiscal incentives that respect maintainer autonomy but carry higher risk of non-binding, symbolic implementation?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence on maintainer behavioral responses to funding conditionality (from other systems or pilot programs) could indicate whether fiscal levers produce measurable equity shifts, but the current record contains no such precedent, so the tradeoff between enforceability and autonomy-respecting design cannot yet be resolved by data alone and partly reflects a governance-philosophy choice.
content.tension: Structural or admissions mandates directly compel behavior change but provoke maintainer-autonomy conflicts and legal friction with church/private institutions, whereas fiscal conditionality avoids direct mandates but has unproven causal effectiveness and risks becoming symbolic rather than binding.
content.title: Direct mandate versus incentive-based governance
value_poles: respect for institutional autonomy and reduced legal conflict
